content.body: Organic chemistry is the study of carbon-based compounds. Carbon is unique — it can form 4 bonds and chain with other carbon atoms to create millions of different molecules. Most compounds in living things and fuels are organic.

Hydrocarbons contain only Carbon and Hydrogen.

ALKANES (saturated — only single bonds):
General formula: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂
Methane: CH₄, Ethane: C₂H₆, Propane: C₃H₈, Butane: C₄H₁₀

ALKENES (unsaturated — contain a C=C double bond):
General formula: CₙH₂ₙ
Ethene: C₂H₄, Propene: C₃H₆, Butene: C₄H₈

content.title: Properties of Alkanes
content.body:
• Good fuels (combust in oxygen releasing CO₂, H₂O and lots of energy)
• Less reactive than alkenes
• Complete combustion: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O
• Incomplete combustion (limited O₂): produces CO (poisonous) and soot (carbon)

Crude oil is a mixture of alkanes separated by fractional distillation.

content.title: Properties and Reactions of Alkenes
content.body:
Alkenes are more reactive than alkanes due to the C=C double bond.

Addition reactions: the double bond opens and atoms add across it.
Hydration: C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH (ethanol — making alcohol!)
Hydrogenation: C₂H₄ + H₂ → C₂H₆ (making margarine from vegetable oil)
Halogenation: C₂H₄ + Br₂ → C₂H₄Br₂ (decolourises bromine water — TEST for alkene!)

Test for alkenes: bromine water (orange/yellow solution).
• Alkene: bromine decolourises (turns colourless) — double bond reacts with Br₂
• Alkane: no colour change

Polymerisation: many small alkene molecules (monomers) join to form a long-chain polymer.
Ethene → poly(ethene) = polythene/polyethylene. Used in plastic bags, bottles.

questions.question: What is the general formula for alkanes?
questions.explanation: Alkanes follow CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. E.g. n=1: CH₄ (methane), n=2: C₂H₆ (ethane).

questions.question: What distinguishes alkenes from alkanes?
options: Alkenes have a carbon-carbon double bond (C=C)
questions.answer: Alkenes have a carbon-carbon double bond (C=C)
questions.explanation: Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one C=C double bond.

questions.question: The molecular formula of propane is:
questions.explanation: Propane: n=3 in CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ = C₃H₈.

questions.question: How do you test for an alkene?
questions.explanation: Alkenes decolourise orange/yellow bromine water because the C=C double bond reacts with Br₂ in an addition reaction.

questions.question: Complete combustion of methane produces:
questions.explanation: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. Complete combustion (plenty of oxygen) always gives CO₂ and H₂O.

questions.question: Incomplete combustion produces the dangerous gas:
questions.explanation: Incomplete combustion (limited oxygen) produces carbon monoxide (CO) — a colourless, odourless, toxic gas.

questions.question: Ethene + Br₂ → ?
questions.explanation: Addition reaction: C₂H₄ + Br₂ → C₂H₄Br₂ (1,2-dibromoethane). The double bond opens and bromine adds across it.

questions.question: Polymerisation of ethene produces:
questions.explanation: Many ethene (C₂H₄) monomers join in addition polymerisation to form poly(ethene) = polythene.

questions.question: Ethene + H₂O → (fermentation catalyst, high pressure)
questions.explanation: Hydration of ethene: C₂H₄ + H₂O → C₂H₅OH (ethanol). This is the industrial method of making alcohol.

questions.question: Crude oil is separated into fractions by:
questions.explanation: Crude oil (a mixture of hydrocarbons with different boiling points) is separated by fractional distillation in a fractionating column.

content.body: The rate of reaction is how fast a chemical reaction occurs — how quickly reactants are converted to products. Understanding and controlling reaction rates is crucial in industrial chemistry, medicine, and food preservation.

Rate of reaction = change in amount / time

Measured by:
• Rate of disappearance of a reactant
• Rate of appearance of a product
• Change in mass, volume of gas, colour, or turbidity over time

content.title: Factors Affecting Rate of Reaction
content.body:
1. CONCENTRATION: More reactant particles → more frequent collisions → faster rate

2. TEMPERATURE: Higher temperature → particles have more kinetic energy → move faster → more frequent AND more energetic collisions

3. SURFACE AREA: Smaller particle size → greater surface area → more particles exposed → faster rate. Powders react faster than lumps.

4. CATALYST: A substance that speeds up a reaction without being used up. Lowers activation energy. Enzymes are biological catalysts.

5. PRESSURE (gases only): Higher pressure → molecules closer together → more collisions → faster rate

content.title: Collision Theory
content.body:
For a reaction to occur, particles must:
1. Collide with each other
2. Have sufficient energy (≥ activation energy)

Activation Energy: the minimum energy needed for a collision to result in a reaction.

A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with LOWER activation energy → more collisions are successful → faster reaction.

Practical example — marble chips + hydrochloric acid:
CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂
Rate can be measured by: volume of CO₂ produced per minute, or decrease in mass of reaction flask.
Using powder (more surface area) or concentrated acid (higher concentration) increases the rate.

questions.question: Which factor does NOT increase the rate of a reaction?
questions.explanation: Decreasing concentration means fewer particles in a given volume → fewer collisions → slower rate.

questions.question: Why does powdering a solid increase the reaction rate?
options: Powders have greater surface area for collisions
questions.answer: Powders have greater surface area for collisions
questions.explanation: Smaller particles expose more surface area for reactant particles to collide with.

questions.question: A catalyst:
options: Is used up in the reaction
Speeds up reaction without being consumed
questions.answer: Speeds up reaction without being consumed
questions.explanation: A catalyst speeds up a reaction and can be recovered unchanged at the end.

questions.question: Activation energy is:
options: Minimum energy needed for a reaction to occur
questions.answer: Minimum energy needed for a reaction to occur
questions.explanation: Activation energy is the energy barrier — collisions must have at least this much energy to result in a reaction.

questions.question: How does increasing temperature increase reaction rate?
options: Particles have more energy → faster and more frequent collisions
questions.answer: Particles have more energy → faster and more frequent collisions
questions.explanation: At higher temperatures, particles move faster (more kinetic energy), collide more often and with greater force.

questions.question: Enzymes are:
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts produced by living organisms that speed up biochemical reactions.

questions.question: Which graph shape shows a typical reaction rate over time?
options: Starts fast then levels off as reactants are used up
questions.answer: Starts fast then levels off as reactants are used up
questions.explanation: As reactants are used up, concentration decreases → fewer collisions → rate decreases → graph levels off when reaction stops.

questions.question: Marble chips react with HCl. Using higher concentration HCl will:
options: Increase the rate of CO₂ production
questions.answer: Increase the rate of CO₂ production
questions.explanation: Higher HCl concentration means more H⁺ ions → more frequent collisions with marble → faster rate.

questions.question: A catalyst works by:
questions.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy, allowing more collisions to be successful.

questions.question: How can you measure the rate of reaction CaCO₃ + HCl → CO₂?
options: Measure decrease in mass of flask
questions.answer: Measure decrease in mass of flask
questions.explanation: CO₂ gas escapes the flask, causing the mass to decrease. Rate = decrease in mass per unit time.
